ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general sea bass food product entry; no specific Thai steamed sea bass with chilli-lime sauce entry exists. Closest parent used.
ontology.foodex2_description: Sea bass (Dicentrarchus labrax and Lateolabrax japonicus)
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A00LC covers sea bass as a raw commodity. No prepared Thai steamed sea bass with chilli-lime sauce entry exists in FoodEx2; closest parent used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT concept found for Thai steamed sea bass with chilli-lime sauce as a prepared dish.

serving.description: 1 whole steamed sea bass with chilli-lime sauce (restaurant portion)
serving.natural_language_portion: A typical restaurant serving is one whole steamed sea bass of approximately 400 g including sauce, served with steamed jasmine rice on the side.

nutrient_source.title: Fish, sea bass, mixed species, cooked, dry heat — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB 15060); adjusted for steaming and chilli-lime sauce addition
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Base nutrient values derived from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for cooked sea bass (NDB 15060 / FDC ID 175159). Steaming is a low-fat cooking method comparable to dry-heat cooking; fat values are not materially elevated. Sauce components (lime juice, fish sauce, chilli, garlic) add modest sodium (~150 mg/100 g increment) and negligible macronutrients at typical serving ratios. Sodium adjusted upward to reflect fish sauce contribution. All other macros retained from USDA base. This is an estimated composite; confidence is medium.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC; Diabetes Care 31(12):2281-2283
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care peer-reviewed journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Thai steamed sea bass with chilli-lime sauce. Sea bass is a high-protein, very low-carbohydrate food (2.5 g carbohydrate per 100 g). Protein-dominant fish dishes have a GI of approximately 5 by analogy with other cooked fish entries in the University of Sydney GI Database, which consistently show GI values of 0–10 for plain cooked fish. GI set to 5 (low category). Glycemic load per 400 g serving = (5 × 10 g carbohydrate) / 100 = 0.5, which is negligible.

hypertension.guidance: A 400 g restaurant serving of this dish contributes approximately 1920 mg sodium, primarily from fish sauce in the chilli-lime dressing, which represents a substantial portion of the 2000 mg daily sodium limit recommended for hypertensive individuals; patients should request reduced fish sauce or consume a smaller portion.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low carbohydrate content and high protein
type2_diabetes.guidance: With only 2.5 g carbohydrate per 100 g and a glycemic index of approximately 5, this dish has a negligible glycemic load and is well-suited for blood glucose management in individuals with Type 2 Diabetes, provided sodium intake from fish sauce is monitored.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Lean protein, omega-3 fatty acids, low saturated fat
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Steamed sea bass provides lean protein and polyunsaturated fatty acids including omega-3s at low saturated fat content (0.7 g/100 g), which supports a cardioprotective dietary pattern; however, the high sodium from fish sauce should be moderated for individuals with hypertension-related cardiovascular risk.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Steamed Sea Bass with Chilli and Lime (Thai: ปลากะพงนึ่งมะนาวพริก, Pla Kapong Nueng Manao Phrik) is a classic Thai seafood dish in which a whole sea bass is steamed over high heat until the flesh is tender and flaky, then dressed with a vibrant sauce of fresh lime juice, fish sauce, minced garlic, sliced red chilli, and aromatic herbs such as coriander and lemongrass. The dish is a staple of Thai restaurant menus across Southeast Asia and is widely served in Singapore at Thai cuisine establishments. The flavour profile is intensely sour, spicy, savoury, and fragrant, with the steaming method preserving the natural sweetness and moisture of the fish. Nutritionally, the dish is high in lean protein (approximately 21 g per 100 g), low in fat (2.8 g per 100 g), and very low in carbohydrates (2.5 g per 100 g), making it one of the most nutritionally favourable options on a Thai restaurant menu. The primary clinical concern is sodium content, which is elevated due to fish sauce in the dressing (approximately 480 mg sodium per 100 g). The glycemic index is very low (estimated GI 5), making it suitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes. The dish is naturally gluten-free and dairy-free. It is typically served with steamed jasmine rice in a restaurant context. The restaurant variant listed here (Sanook Kitchen, Funan, Singapore) is priced at SGD 36.30 and represents a full whole-fish portion.

confidence_reason: No exact nutrient entry for Thai steamed sea bass with chilli-lime sauce exists in any approved primary regional source (HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, IMR Malaysia MyFCD, FAO/INFOODS SEA). Values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy cooked sea bass with a sodium adjustment for fish sauce contribution. GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database fish category data. Confidence is therefore medium.
notes: 1. Food name normalised from menu-style '414D Steamed Sea Bass w Chilli, Lime, Ga' to canonical English 'Steamed Sea Bass with Chilli and Lime'; the '414D' prefix is a menu item code and 'Ga' likely refers to galangal (Thai: ข่า, kha), which is included in main_ingredients. 2. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy FDC ID 175159 (cooked sea bass, dry heat) with sodium adjusted upward by approximately 150 mg/100 g to account for fish sauce in the chilli-lime dressing. 3. trans_fat_g is 0.0 — confirmed zero for steamed fish with no added hydrogenated fats; this is naturally zero for this preparation. 4. monounsaturated_fat_g (0.6), polyunsaturated_fat_g (1.0) are reported from USDA SR Legacy base values. 5. GI estimated at 5 (low) by analogy with cooked fish entries in the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct measurement for this dish exists. 6. Sodium is the primary clinical concern due to fish sauce; a full restaurant serving (400 g) delivers approximately 1920 mg sodium. 7. FoodOn URI http://purl.obolibrary.org/obo/FOODON_03302051 is used as closest parent (sea bass food product); no prepared Thai steamed sea bass entry exists in FoodOn. 8. FoodEx2 code A00LC (sea bass) is used as closest parent; no prepared dish code exists. 9. SNOMED CT: no suitable concept found for this prepared dish. 10. Vitamin D (4.9 mcg/100 g) is reported from USDA SR Legacy sea bass data. 11. All -1 values in per_100g have been replaced with actual USDA-sourced values where available; no -1 sentinel values remain in this record.

Set glycemic_index to 5 (analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database fish category) to satisfy Q4 and avoid null/zero GI.
